dataSource: City of San Diego Open Data Portal - Operating Budget (FY2026 Adopted)

departments.name: Police
departments.description: Patrol, investigations, traffic enforcement, and community policing across 9 divisions. Largest discretionary department in the General Fund.
departments.context: San Diego has one of the highest police-to-resident ratios among major California cities.

departments.name: Fire-Rescue
departments.description: Fire suppression, emergency medical services, lifeguard operations, and wildfire prevention across 47 fire stations.
departments.context: Wildfire risk has increased costs significantly — fire prevention programs doubled since 2020.

departments.name: Parks & Recreation
departments.description: Maintenance of 340+ parks, recreation centers, pools, trails, canyons, and open space. The proposed budget includes reduced maintenance hours.
departments.context: Parks serve as heat refuges and mental health resources — disproportionately used by lower-income neighborhoods.

departments.name: Transportation & Streets
departments.description: Street maintenance, traffic signals, sidewalk repair. SD has a $3B+ deferred maintenance backlog.
departments.context: Every dollar cut today adds ~$7 in future repair costs due to deferred street maintenance.

departments.name: Homelessness Strategies
departments.description: Outreach teams, shelter operations, housing navigation, and encampment response. Coordinates with county and nonprofits.
departments.context: San Diego has the 4th largest unhoused population in the US. Shelter costs have risen 40% since 2021.

departments.name: Library
departments.description: 36 branch libraries plus Central Library — books, digital resources, programs for children, seniors, job seekers, and new residents.
departments.context: Libraries are the only truly free public space in most neighborhoods. Branch closures disproportionately affect renters and seniors.

departments.name: Stormwater
departments.description: Flood control infrastructure, stormwater treatment, and MS4 permit compliance. Heavy rain events have exposed major infrastructure gaps.
departments.context: Recent storms revealed $500M+ in deferred stormwater infrastructure needs.

departments.name: Citywide & Administrative
departments.description: Debt service, City Attorney, City Treasurer, Finance, HR, IT, and other central services that support all departments.
departments.context: Includes pension obligations — the city's pension debt is ~$3B and growing.

departments.name: Other Departments
departments.description: Development Services, Economic Development, City Planning, General Services, Real Estate, and other City functions.
departments.context: Includes permitting (which funds itself via fees) and economic development programs.

revenues.name: Property Tax
revenues.description: State-mandated 1% property tax — SD receives ~17% of the countywide levy. Cannot be increased without a 2/3 voter supermajority.

revenues.name: Sales Tax
revenues.description: City receives 1% of the 8.75% sales tax. A 0.25% increase generates ~$90M/year.

revenues.name: Hotel Tax (TOT)
revenues.description: Transient Occupancy Tax on hotel stays. Currently 10.5%. San Diego is a major tourism destination.

options.label: +1% hotel tax (+$17M/yr)
options.note: Council can approve without voter vote

revenues.name: User Fee Recovery
revenues.description: Many city services are provided below cost. Increasing cost recovery on permits and recreation programs could raise revenue.

options.label: Increase cost recovery (+$25M/yr)
options.note: Shifts costs to direct service users

revenues.name: Cannabis Tax
revenues.description: Tax on cannabis retailers. Current rate is 8%. Raising it could generate modest additional revenue.

options.label: Increase rate (+$8M/yr)
options.note: Risk of pushing buyers to unregulated market

civicActions.label: Contact Your Council Member
civicActions.description: Find and email your district representative directly.

civicActions.label: Speak at a Budget Hearing
civicActions.description: The Council holds public hearings in May and June. Sign up to speak on the record.

civicActions.label: Read the Full Proposed Budget
civicActions.description: Download and read the complete FY2027 proposed budget document.

civicActions.label: Explore the Open Data Portal
civicActions.description: All spending and revenue data used in this tool comes from the City's open data portal.
